title: What is cognition?
blurb: Definition, everyday examples, why “ordinary” behavior is extraordinary.

steps.body:
Definition
Cognition = acquisition and processing of sensory information about the world to make behavioral decisions — the sum of intelligent mental activities (not just breathing/digestion).

Everyday example: reading
Device recognition → memory for controls → focused vision → language + concepts → learning into STM/LTM → goals/decisions. One “simple” act coordinates many subsystems.

Field stance
Cognition mostly studies how things go right. No complete unified theory yet — progress is real but incomplete and debated.

choices: Acquiring/processing sensory info to decide how to act

steps.q: The textbook’s aim is mainly to:
options: Introduce questions, methods, and key results of mind science

title: Why study cognition?
blurb: Basic vs applied research; human factors; AI; education/health.

steps.body:
Basic research
Understand who we are and how cognition works — not required to have a product, though applications often follow.

Applied research
End goal is a solution: disorders, education design, human factors interfaces, building AI systems.

steps.prompt: Designing cockpit displays using attention research is:
choices: Only basic pure theory with no use

steps.q: AI in this chapter is framed as:
options: Building machines that perform (some) intelligent tasks
steps.explain: Engineering/CS branch linked to understanding intelligence.

title: AI as a mirror of understanding
blurb: Feynman test, Deep Blue, IFTT limits, open-world hardness.

steps.body:
Feynman test
“What I cannot create I do not understand.” Building working models gauges true understanding of mind/brain.

Hard vs easy for machines
Humans: easy common sense, language, messy rooms.
Machines historically: fast formal calculation, constrained factory/chess search.

Deep Blue
Massive search + simple ops — not a general mind. Winning chess ≠ solving everyday intelligence.

IFTT / early chatbots
Work when conditions are pre-listed. Fail when the world is open-ended. String matching ≠ understanding.

choices: The world is fully open and novel always

steps.explain: Brute-force-style excellence in a formal game.

steps.body:
Three prongs
1) Cognitive psychology — behavior experiments to infer processes.
2) Neuroscience — brain structures/activity/mechanisms.
3) Computational modeling — explicit runnable theories.

Why all three?
Each constrains the others. Behavior alone underdetermines mechanism; brains alone need functional theory; models force precision.

steps.prompt: The three broad approaches named in Ch1 include:

options: Infers mental processes from measured behavior

steps.body:
CNS = brain + spinal cord.
PNS = nerves outside CNS.

Somatic ≈ voluntary skeletal control + body senses.
Autonomic ≈ involuntary organs; sympathetic (fight/flight) vs parasympathetic (rest/digest).

title: Cortex anatomy & function
blurb: Lobes, maps, methods for functional division.

steps.body:
Lobes
Occipital: vision.
Temporal: audition/language/memory aspects.
Parietal: somatosensory/spatial.
Frontal: motor + executive.

Functional mapping tools
Lesions, stimulation, electrophysiology, neuroimaging — converging evidence. Double dissociations support separable systems.

options: BOLD blood-oxygen signals related to activity

title: Neurons & ANNs
blurb: Spikes, synapses, feature detectors; artificial nets as models.

steps.body:
Neuron basics
Dendrites receive, axons send, synapses release transmitters, action potentials are all-or-none spikes. Feature detectors prefer specific stimulus properties.

ANNs
Units + weights + learning. Not full brains, but useful computational models (and engineering tools). Watch for overfitting vs generalization.

Sensation: physical energy → neural signals (transduction).
Perception: organizing/interpreting those signals into useful experience.
Same input can yield different percepts (context, knowledge). Illusions reveal the system’s assumptions.

choices: Converting stimulus energy to neural signals

options: A constructive interpretation of sensory data

steps.body:
Bottom-up = data-driven features.
Top-down = knowledge/expectations/context.

Gestalt: organization laws (proximity, similarity, closure…); whole ≠ mere sum.
Unconscious inference (Helmholtz): best guesses about the world from incomplete data.

choices: Only pure receptors with no knowledge

title: Vision & CNNs
blurb: Depth, constancy, recognition hierarchy; convolutional nets.

steps.body: Vision builds features → objects. Depth: monocular cues + binocular disparity. Constancies stabilize object properties across conditions.
CNNs: hierarchical local filters (convolution) + pooling — engineering success on vision tasks, loosely inspired by visual hierarchy.

title: Attention filters
blurb: Early vs late selection, attenuation, dichotic listening.

steps.body:
Early selection (Broadbent): filter on physical features before full meaning.
Late selection: much analysis before selection.
Attenuation (Treisman): unattended turned down, not fully blocked — names can break through.
Dichotic listening + shadowing is the classic method. Cocktail party effect challenges pure complete early blocking of meaning.

steps.prompt: Treisman attenuation says unattended info is:
choices: Reduced but not always zero; important items can pass

options: Physical/sensory channel properties before deep meaning

title: Load, dividing, purpose of attention
blurb: Lavie load, dual-task costs, FIT/binding, endogenous vs exogenous.

steps.body: Load theory: high perceptual load uses capacity → fewer distractor effects; low load leaves spare capacity for distractors.
Divided attention: dual-task costs; practice/automaticity can free resources.

Feature integration: attention binds features into objects; illusory conjunctions when overloaded.
Endogenous = voluntary; exogenous = stimulus-driven capture. You need both.

choices: Only a loud bang capturing you

steps.q: Illusory conjunctions support that attention is needed to:
options: Detect any single feature always without binding

title: Neural attention & ADHD
blurb: Networks, sensory gain, ADHD pharmacology theme.

steps.body: Frontoparietal control systems modulate sensory cortex (gain on attended inputs). Disorders like neglect show spatial attention failures.
ADHD involves attention control difficulties. Stimulants (e.g., methylphenidate class) increase DA/NE availability — clinical decisions weigh severity and side effects (not first-line freestyle).

steps.prompt: ADHD meds like Ritalin classically increase:
steps.reveal: Catecholamine availability → focus support for many patients.

title: Ch2 map: How to study cognition
blurb: Mind–brain, history, cognitive approach, methods — your existing bank lives here.

steps.body: You already have a strong Ch2 bank (mind–body, structuralism, behaviorism, cognitive revolution, Donders/Stroop, methods, complementary neuroscience).
Study order: Mind & Brain → Brain & Behavior → Structuralism → Behaviorism → Cognitive Revolution → Cognitive Approach → Methods → Neuroscience.

choices: Mental events relate to physical brain processes

options: Fully open and measured by introspection only
A black box; study S–R only
